core_argument: The entertainment industry is undergoing a structural reorganization driven by three converging forces: the exhaustion of traditional IP pipelines, the migration of audiences to creator-driven platforms via superior algorithmic discovery, and the emergence of creator-branded content as the only reliable bridge for bringing those audiences back to scripted entertainment. This restructuring is not optional — it is being forced by the simultaneous depletion of traditional IP sources and the migration of audiences to platforms with fundamentally different content economics.

supply_exhaustion.summary: Traditional IP pipelines — books, comics, video games, songs, theater, journalism, life rights, board games — are showing signs of saturation. Option prices have inflated, hit rates are declining, and the volume of viable material is insufficient to meet streaming-era demand. The traditional development model was designed for scarcity (limited broadcast slots); the streaming environment demands volume that these pipelines cannot sustain.
evidence: Book option prices have inflated dramatically as studios compete for shrinking pool of properties with built-in audiences
Comic book adaptation fatigue evidenced by declining box office returns for non-tentpole superhero properties
Video game adaptation pipeline reaching historic density (6 theatrical releases in 2026) suggesting rapid mining of the category
Netflix walked away from $82.7B WBD acquisition (entire DC, Harry Potter, HBO library), stock rose 8% — market pricing legacy IP as harvest, not growth
Paramount-WBD merger 85% dependent on linear cable profits, confirming traditional pipeline is a harvest play
gaps: Systematic data on book option price inflation over time
Conversion rate from option to production across IP categories
Song/jukebox model pipeline depth — how many catalog properties are genuinely adaptable?
Board game/toy IP pipeline beyond Barbie — replicable or one-off?

demand_migration.summary: Audiences have migrated from scripted content to creator-driven platforms, pulled not primarily by content preference but by radically superior algorithmic discovery infrastructure. YouTube and TikTok match audiences to content individually and continuously; streamers use blunt-instrument category browsing. The migration is a discovery infrastructure problem, not a content quality problem.
evidence: YouTube accounts for 11.1% of all TV streaming, surpassing Netflix at 8.5%
Streaming hit all-time high of 47.5% TV share in December 2025
Gen Z daily TV viewing at 36% vs. 73% for Boomers
YouTube has 1 billion monthly active podcast viewers, 700M hours of living room podcast viewing per month
72% of podcast listeners prefer shows with video (Cumulus Media study)
US advertising spend on creators projected at $37B in 2025, up from $13.9B four years prior — 4x rate of broader media industry growth
gaps: Direct measurement of audience migration patterns by demo
Evidence on whether creator-branded shows have different retention curves
Whether platforms are using creator audience data to improve recommendation engines
Measurement of the algorithmic discovery gap between YouTube/TikTok and streaming platforms

discovery_bridge.summary: Creator-branded content may be the only reliable mechanism for bringing algorithmically-sorted audiences back into scripted consumption. The value of the creator brand isn't the IP itself — it's the targeting that the creator's audience relationship provides for free, which would cost streamers millions in marketing and which their own recommendation infrastructure cannot replicate.
evidence: Netflix committing to 50-75 original video podcast shows for 2026 launch
Netflix-Spotify deal and Netflix-iHeartMedia deal pulling podcast content off YouTube as competitive play
Wondery's systematic podcast-to-TV pipeline: Dirty John, Dr. Death, Shrink Next Door, WeCrashed all successful adaptations
Audiochuck $250M valuation and Chernin investment funding creator-to-scripted expansion
Matt Shanfield hired from Sony nonfiction to build Audiochuck TV/film vertical
Netflix video podcast economics: $5K-50K per episode vs. $3-10M for scripted hour
57% of new streaming subscribers choosing ad-supported tiers, creating volume demand creator content fills
gaps: Conversion data: do podcast audiences actually follow adaptations to screen?
What development executives actually do at creator-native companies vs. traditional studios
How creator-driven companies protect IP/brand differently than traditional licensors
The economics of creator-driven audience acquisition vs. traditional marketing spend
Whether the proof-of-concept model (podcast audience as market validation) actually reduces development risk in practice

books.notes: Option price inflation, declining conversion rates, pool shrinking after decades of mining

comics.notes: Marvel/DC tentpole fatigue. Independent comics and manga potentially undertapped

video_games.notes: 6 theatrical releases in 2026. Last of Us, Fallout successful. Pipeline depth unclear

songs_jukebox.notes: Bohemian Rhapsody, Rocketman proved model. Piña Colada in development. Largely untapped catalog

theater.notes: Hamilton model works but limited pipeline. Adaptation flows both directions

journalism.notes: Pipeline evolved from magazine features to podcast-driven true crime

life_rights.notes: Podcast layer gives new life but may be repackaging same material

board_games_toys.notes: Barbie proved model but replicability undemonstrated

podcasts.notes: Audiochuck, Wondery, Spotify, iHeartMedia. Systematic adaptation pipelines emerging

youtube.notes: MrBeast-to-Netflix model. Creator-to-studio deals increasing. YouTube at 11.1% TV streaming share

tiktok.notes: Regulatory uncertainty complicates. Talent discovery mechanism is platform-agnostic

newsletters.notes: Optionist model, Free Press/Paramount. Small audiences but niche/prestige opportunity

livestreaming.notes: Markiplier Iron Lung, Kai Cenat crossover plays. Gaming-to-entertainment pipeline forming

development_function.summary: The existing literature says almost nothing about what happens when a creator-led company needs to professionalize its development function. This is the thesis's original analytical territory.
key_questions: What does the development executive's job look like at Audiochuck vs. at a traditional studio?
How do creator-driven companies protect IP and brand differently than traditional licensors?
How should creator-driven IP be valued (audience relationship depth vs. comparable sales)?
What creative control structures are needed when the creator IS the brand?
Where do the executives come from who can operate at the intersection of creator-native and institutional entertainment?

tracked_executives.name: Aaron Hart
tracked_executives.title: Head of TV and Film
tracked_executives.track_record: Dirty John, Dr. Death, Shrink Next Door, Joe vs. Carole, WeCrashed

claims.claim: The entertainment industry's restructuring is driven by the convergence of supply exhaustion, demand migration, and the discovery bridge — not by any single factor in isolation.
Creator-branded content functions as audience targeting infrastructure, not just content. The value is in the discovery mechanism, not the IP itself.
The development function at creator-native companies is fundamentally different from traditional studios and requires a new kind of executive who can bridge both worlds.

Traditional IP pipelines are not declining uniformly — some categories (video games, songs) are accelerating while others (books, comics) show saturation. The overall pipeline volume is insufficient for streaming demand regardless.

Audience migration from scripted to creator platforms is driven primarily by superior algorithmic discovery, not by content quality decline. This means the solution is a discovery bridge, not better content.

chapter_outline.title: The Pipeline That Built Hollywood
chapter_outline.focus: Historical IP origination model — books, comics, specs, licensing. Why it worked for decades. What sustained it.

chapter_outline.title: The Exhaustion
chapter_outline.focus: Supply exhaustion across traditional IP categories. Option economics. Hit rate decline. Volume insufficiency.

chapter_outline.title: The Great Migration
chapter_outline.focus: Audience movement to creator platforms. Algorithmic discovery vs. institutional curation. Demo-specific patterns.

chapter_outline.title: The YouTube Precedent
chapter_outline.focus: 15 years of creator ecosystem evolution. MCN rise and collapse. What survived. MrBeast model.

chapter_outline.title: The Audio Bridge
chapter_outline.focus: Podcast-to-scripted pipeline. Wondery, Audiochuck, Spotify. The proof-of-concept model.

chapter_outline.title: The New Development Executive
chapter_outline.focus: What the job looks like at creator-native companies. Brand protection. Valuation. Creative control. Case studies.

chapter_outline.title: The Discovery Problem
chapter_outline.focus: Why streamers can't find audiences for their own content. Creator brands as targeting infrastructure.

chapter_outline.title: What Comes Next
chapter_outline.focus: Emerging channels. Video games, songs, newsletters. The next wave of IP origination.
